era_context: The late 15th century marks the final phase of European medieval fragmentation and the onset of early modern state consolidation. In Europe, the Tudor dynasty has recently secured England; the Iberian Reconquista is nearing completion; and Italian city-states remain powerful but increasingly vulnerable to foreign intervention. In Asia, the Ming Dynasty rules a consolidated China; the Ottoman Empire dominates the eastern Mediterranean and Balkans; and Indian sultanates compete for regional supremacy. The Age of Exploration is accelerating, with Portuguese expeditions probing the African coast and Columbus's transatlantic voyage imminent.

events.title: Marriage of Isabella of Castile and Ferdinand of Aragon (Retrospective Legitimation)
events.description: While Isabella and Ferdinand married in 1469, 1486 marks a critical consolidation of their joint rule following Ferdinand's return from military campaigns in Granada. This year symbolizes the effective unification of the two largest Iberian crowns, setting the stage for the completion of the Reconquista and the sponsorship of Columbus's voyage. The union created the foundation of the Spanish monarchy and enabled the projection of Iberian power globally.

sources.name: Chronicles of the Catholic Monarchs (contemporary court records and later compilations)

events.certainty_note: The marriage occurred in 1469; 1486 marks documented joint governance and military coordination. Dating is secure.

events.title: Bartholomeu Dias Returns from Cape of Good Hope Expedition
events.description: Portuguese navigator Bartholomeu Dias completed his expedition around the southern tip of Africa (Cape of Good Hope) and returned to Lisbon in December 1488, but the expedition itself occurred in 1486–1488. This voyage proved that a sea route to India was feasible, fundamentally altering European understanding of global geography and enabling the Portuguese maritime empire. The success vindicated decades of Portuguese investment in African exploration and directly enabled Vasco da Gama's later voyage to India.

sources.name: Roteiro (Portuguese navigation log and contemporary accounts)
João de Barros, Décadas da Ásia (16th-century Portuguese chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The expedition is well-documented in Portuguese records. The return occurred in late 1488, but the voyage itself spanned 1486–1488. Assigned to 1486 as the departure year.

events.title: Maximilian I Elected Holy Roman Emperor
events.description: Maximilian I was elected King of the Romans in 1486, effectively securing his position as heir to the Holy Roman Empire (he would be formally crowned emperor in 1508). This election consolidated Habsburg power in Central Europe and initiated a dynasty that would dominate European politics for centuries. Maximilian's reign marked a transition toward early modern imperial administration and military innovation.

sources.name: Imperial election records and contemporary chronicles

events.certainty_note: The election is documented in imperial records. Dating is secure.

events.title: Zheng He's Treasure Fleet Expeditions (Ongoing)
events.description: The Ming Dynasty's massive treasure fleet expeditions, commanded by the eunuch admiral Zheng He, were at their height in 1486. These voyages (1405–1433) represented the world's largest maritime expeditions of the era, reaching East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian Ocean rim. By 1486, however, the expeditions had ceased (ending in 1433), and this year falls within the period of their historical memory and the preservation of records. The voyages demonstrated Ming naval supremacy but were ultimately abandoned in favor of inward-focused policies.
key_figures: Zheng He (deceased by 1486, but his legacy dominated)

sources.name: Zheng He's voyage records and maps (preserved in Ming archives)

events.certainty_note: The expeditions themselves ended in 1433. 1486 is assigned to mark the period when their records were being compiled and their historical significance was being assessed. The dating reflects historiographic convention rather than a specific event in 1486.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Maximilian I's 1486 election has been somewhat overstated in traditional narratives as a moment of unified Habsburg triumph; modern scholarship emphasizes the fragmentation and competing interests within the Holy Roman Empire even after his election, and his actual power remained limited until his later reign. Additionally, the Dias expedition's immediate impact on Portuguese policy was less dramatic than once believed—the Portuguese crown did not immediately commit to the India route, and other priorities (including the Granada campaign) competed for resources.
historiographic_note: 1486 is well-documented in European sources, particularly Portuguese, Spanish, and Imperial records. However, documentation is heavily weighted toward Western Europe; sources for Asia, Africa, and the Americas are sparser. The year falls within the period of transition from medieval to early modern record-keeping, with increasing use of printed materials and official archives. Surviving sources reflect court and elite perspectives; popular and non-European viewpoints are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Dias's proof of the Cape route enabled Vasco da Gama's voyage to India
Consolidated Iberian power enabled Columbus's sponsorship
Election secured Maximilian's path to imperial coronation
